dashboard.label: Onboarding Guide
dashboard.description: This guide will help you onboard OT and production data into Splunk and map it to the OilGas data model.

div.p: This onboarding guide is the app's first-run setup experience. Review the steps below, then click Mark App Setup Complete once the app is ready for your users. AI configuration is optional and can be completed later from AI Config.
div.div: Use this button after you finish the onboarding steps below. It sets the Splunk app's configured flag so the onboarding guide is no longer forced on launch.

✅ Demo-ready with one short setup step. The OilGas Splunk App ships with dashboards, searches, AI features, and sample data files for the demo generator. To start generating demo data, create the oil_gas index and then enable the scripted input.

div.p: The app expects an index named oil_gas for the bundled searches, dashboards, and demo data generator. Create this index in Splunk before enabling the scripted input.

tr.td: Stores all oil & gas well telemetry events (production volumes, pressures, temperatures, ESP metrics)

div.div:
⚠️ Planning to connect your own data source?
If you configure your own Data Input (e.g., HEC, Forwarder, OPC UA), keep the demo event generator disabled to avoid mixing real and synthetic data in the oil_gas index.
Go to Settings > Data Inputs > Scripts, find event_generator.py, and enable it only when you want demo telemetry.
div.p: ⚙️ Manage oil_gas index in Splunk | 📖 Splunk Docs: Managing Indexes

A search macro named oil_gas_indexes is pre-defined in the app. It expands to index=oil_gas and is used throughout all app searches and dashboards, so that the target index can be changed in one place without modifying every saved search.
div.div: How it works: In any SPL search, writing `oil_gas_indexes` (with backticks) will be automatically replaced with index=oil_gas at search time. This is the standard Splunk pattern for making searches portable and easy to reconfigure.

pre.code: | Example usage in SPL: `oil_gas_indexes` sourcetype=oilgas | stats avg(AVG_DOWNHOLE_PRESSURE) by WELL_BORE_CODE

div.p: ⚙️ Edit oil_gas_indexes macro | 📖 Splunk Docs: Search Macros

Located at bin/event_generator.py, this script acts as a Scripted Input in Splunk. Once enabled, it continuously generates realistic oil & gas well telemetry events every 5 minutes and writes them to stdout, which Splunk captures and indexes into the oil_gas index.
Each cycle (every 5 minutes), the script processes all 7 configured wells in parallel:

ol.li: Reads well CSV data from samples/wells_data/ — one CSV file per well. The script finds the row matching the current day and month, simulating real-time daily production data replay. Rows are cached to avoid re-reading the file within the same day.

Normalizes numeric fields — removes comma separators and converts to float. For time-dependent production volume fields (e.g., BORE_OIL_VOL, BORE_GAS_VOL), daily totals are scaled down proportionally to a 5-minute interval: batch_value = daily_value / 86400 * 300 # 300 seconds = 5 minutes
Adds ±3% random variation to each numeric value to simulate realistic sensor noise.

Generates ESP (Electric Submersible Pump) sensor data for production wells (when injection volume BORE_WI_VOL == 0). With a 3% probability per field, values fall into alert ranges — simulating real equipment anomalies.
Writes a JSON event to stdout with _time as the first key. Splunk's scripted input mechanism reads this output and indexes it into oil_gas.
Sleeps 5 minutes, then repeats indefinitely.

div.p: The script simulates 7 real wells from the publicly available Equinor Volve dataset:
The script also supports historical backfill: generate events for a past date range and import them as a file input. This is useful for pre-populating dashboards with historical trends.

pre.code:
# Step 1: Generate historical data to a file /opt/splunk/bin/splunk cmd python \ /opt/splunk/etc/apps/oilgas_splunk_app/bin/event_generator.py \ --start 2025-01-01T00:00:00 \ --end 2025-07-12T00:00:00 \ > /tmp/backfill.txt
# Step 2: Import /tmp/backfill.txt as a Splunk File & Directory Monitor input # Settings > Data Inputs > Files & Directories > Add new

div.p: The script is packaged as a Splunk Scripted Input in default/inputs.conf and is disabled by default until the target index exists. You can view, enable, or disable it in Splunk:
⚙️ Settings > Data Inputs > Scripts | 📖 Splunk Docs: Scripted Inputs
div.div: 💡 Tip: To verify data is flowing, run this search in Splunk: `oil_gas_indexes` | head 10 | table _time, WELL_BORE_CODE, AVG_DOWNHOLE_PRESSURE, BORE_OIL_VOL

div.h3: 🤖 Optional Demo Step: Configure AI Assistant
div.p: After demo data is flowing into oil_gas, you can enable the app's AI assistant for guided investigation on the demo dashboards.

ol.li: Open AI Config.
Choose a provider protocol: OpenAI Compatible, Anthropic Messages, Google Gemini, or Ollama.
Enter the provider endpoint URL, model or deployment name, auth settings, and API key or token.
Click Save, then Test Connection to confirm Splunk can reach the provider.
Open Dynamic KPIs or KPI Details to use the embedded AI chat with the demo dashboard context.

div.div: 🔒 Note: The API key is stored in Splunk password storage, and browser requests go through the app's Splunk REST relay instead of calling the provider directly. No information is shared with SoftServe; app data is processed by the client's Splunk deployment and only the AI endpoint configured by the client's administrator.
⚠️ Network requirement: Remote providers must use https://. Plain HTTP is supported only for local Ollama testing on localhost, 127.0.0.1, or ::1.

ol.li: ✅ App installed
✅ oil_gas index created in Splunk
✅ event_generator.py scripted input enabled if you want demo data
✅ oil_gas_indexes macro configured — all searches use index=oil_gas
Optional: install the sample KPI pack below if you want the KPI dashboard populated immediately
Optional: configure AI Config and run Test Connection
Open any dashboard after data starts flowing
Optional AI dashboards: Dynamic KPIs or KPI Details
To explore fields: Run a quick search

div.h3: 📊 Optional Demo Step: Install Sample KPIs
div.p: The KPI dashboard can be populated with a bundled sample KPI pack. This creates the sample KPI metadata and the corresponding saved searches used by Dynamic KPIs and KPI Details.

ol.li: Click Install Sample KPIs.
Wait for the confirmation message below the button.
Open the KPI dashboard to confirm the sample KPI panels appear.
If needed, refine or remove individual KPIs later in Setup KPI.

div.div: 💡 What this installs: sample records in the kpi_store collection plus their matching kpi_search_* saved searches. You can rerun this only on a clean KPI setup; existing entries may return duplicate or conflict errors.

div.h3: 📥 Section 1: General Onboarding to Splunk for Oil & Gas Data
div.p: This guide will help you get your data into Splunk. See the "Get Data In" architecture diagram below for an overview of the process:

ol.li:
🗄️ Create Indexes Create indexes for your data in Splunk so it can be searched easily. Go to Settings > Indexes and create a new index (e.g., oil_gas).
📖 Official Splunk Documentation: Set up multiple indexes

🌐 Enable and Configure HTTP Event Collector (HEC) Enable HEC and create a token for data ingestion. Go to Settings > Data Inputs > HTTP Event Collector.
📖 Official Splunk Documentation: HTTP Event Collector

🔧 Configure Data Ingestion on Your Devices Configure your data collectors (e.g., EdgeHub, Telegraf, Universal Forwarder, Heavy Forwarder) with the HEC token and index from the previous steps. Refer to your collector's documentation for details.

li.p: Mapping to the OilGas data model may require a combination of normalization techniques:

ul.li:
Field Extraction: Extract WELL_BORE_CODE from the OPC UA node id and AVG_DOWNHOLE_TEMPERATURE from the value using a single extraction with named regex groups.
EXTRACT-well_bore_and_temp = "id\\":\\s*\\"ns=2;s=freeopcua.Tags.(?<WELL_BORE_CODE>[^.]+).temperature\\".*?\\"pump2_temperature\\":\\s*(?<AVG_DOWNHOLE_TEMPERATURE>[\d.]+)"

Field Aliasing: Map raw field names to model fields if needed.
FIELDALIAS-avg_downhole_temp = pump2_temperature AS AVG_DOWNHOLE_TEMPERATURE

Calculation: Use eval to create or transform fields.
EVAL:AVG_DOWNHOLE_TEMPERATURE = round(AVG_DOWNHOLE_TEMPERATURE, 2)

Upload Well Metadata Lookup
Upload the oilgas_assets_wells.csv file containing well metadata to Splunk as a lookup table. Go to Settings > Lookups > Lookup table files and upload your CSV.
FIELD_CODE,FIELD_NAME,WELL_BORE_CODE,WELL_BORE_NAME,WELL_TYPE,FLOW_KIND,LAST_SEEN 123,FIELD1,WELL1,"1/2-F-1",WI,injection,1741343512
After uploading, update the lookup definition with new CSV:
Edit oilgas_assets_wells Lookup Definition | 📖 Splunk Docs: Lookups

li.p: See the OilGas Data Model for the full list of fields to map.

ol.li:
✅ Validate Data Run a search using the OilGas data model to validate your onboarding and field mapping:
| datamodel OilGas Main_Data search Confirm fields are extracted and mapped as expected. Use dashboards to visualize your data.

🤖 Configure MLTK for AI Features (Optional) If using AI features, install and configure the Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK) app version 5.6.0 or later. See the official MLTK installation and configuration guide for detailed setup instructions. Default MLTK configuration is used for AI features in app.

📊 Sample Data in oil_gas Index After you create the oil_gas index, you can enable the bundled event_generator.py scripted input to populate it with sample data for testing and exploration.
You can view or manage this input in Splunk at Settings > Data Inputs > Scripts.
This lets you try searches, dashboards, and field mapping before onboarding your own data.

div.p: The app includes an optional AI assistant panel on dashboards such as Dynamic KPIs and KPI Details. Before users can send prompts, an administrator should configure the provider connection in AI Config.
div.div: 🔒 Security model: The browser sends requests to Splunk only. The provider API key is stored in Splunk password storage, and the backend relay makes the outbound call to the configured provider endpoint. No information is shared with SoftServe; app data is processed by the client's Splunk deployment and only the AI endpoint configured by the client's administrator.

ol.li: ⚙️ Open AI Config Open AI Config from the app navigation or app setup view.
🌐 Choose the Provider Protocol Select the protocol that matches your endpoint: OpenAI Compatible, Anthropic Messages, Google Gemini, or Ollama.

🔗 Enter Connection Settings Fill in the endpoint URL, model or deployment name, auth mode, auth header details, and API key or token. The API key field can be left blank on later edits if you want to keep the stored secret unchanged.
🎛️ Tune Request Options Optionally set request timeout, TLS verification, temperature, max tokens, extra headers, and extra request parameters to match your provider and governance requirements.
💾 Save and Validate Click Save, then Test Connection. If the test fails with 401 or 403, verify the endpoint URL, auth mode, auth header name or prefix, model name, and stored credential.
📊 Use the Embedded AI Experience After the connection test succeeds, open Dynamic KPIs or KPI Details to use the built-in AI chat panel against the data already shown on those dashboards.

div.div: ⚠️ Network requirement: Remote providers must use https://. Plain HTTP is supported only for local Ollama testing on localhost, 127.0.0.1, or ::1.

div.h3: 📡 Section 3: OPC UA + Telegraf Demo Guide

li.div: ▶️ Run Telegraf with OPC UA Input

ol.li:
🗺️ Map to Splunk Data Model Use props.conf and transforms.conf to extract and map fields from the event to your OilGas data model fields.
Example:
FIELDALIAS-avg_whp_p = pump2_pressure AS AVG_WHP_P (maps to Average wellhead pressure)
FIELDALIAS-well_bore_code = device AS WELL_BORE_CODE (maps to Well bore code)
FIELDALIAS-avg_wht_p = pump2_temperature AS AVG_WHT_P (maps to Average wellhead temperature)
See the OilGas Data Model for the full list of fields. 📖 Splunk Docs: props.conf | 📖 Splunk Docs: transforms.conf

div.p: See the README or contact your Splunk administrator for further assistance.
